docs.title: System Administrator Documentation
docs.text: Welcome to the Carambus documentation for system administrators! Here you'll find all information for installing, configuring, and maintaining the system.

docs.title: 🎯 Your Role as System Administrator
docs.text: As a system administrator, you are responsible for: - 🖥️ Installation: Set up and commission the system - ⚙️ Configuration: Adapt system to your requirements - 🔐 Security: Secure system and manage backups - 📊 Monitoring: Monitor performance and detect problems - 🔄 Updates: Keep system current and secure - 🆘 Support: Solve technical problems for users

docs.title: 🚀 Quick Start by Deployment Option
docs.text: Choose your deployment variant:

docs.title: Option 1: Raspberry Pi All-in-One (Recommended for Individual Clubs)
docs.text:
Setup time: 30-60 minutes Difficulty: ⭐ Easy

➡️ Raspberry Pi Quickstart Guide

docs.title: Option 2: Cloud Hosting (Recommended for Federations)
docs.text:
Setup time: 2-4 hours Difficulty: ⭐⭐ Medium

➡️ Installation Overview - Cloud Setup

docs.title: Option 3: On-Premise Server
docs.text:
Setup time: 1-2 days Difficulty: ⭐⭐⭐ Demanding

➡️ Installation Overview - On-Premise

docs.title: 1. Installation
docs.text:
Basic installation: - System requirements - Operating system setup (Ubuntu) - Install dependencies - Deploy Carambus - Initial configuration

➡️ Complete Installation Guide

Special installations: - Raspberry Pi Setup: All-in-One kiosk system - Raspberry Pi Client: Display/Scoreboard only - Database Setup: Configure PostgreSQL

docs.title: 2. Configuration
docs.text:
System settings: - Configure club data - Set up email server - SSL/TLS certificates - Backup strategies

➡️ Email Configuration

Scoreboard setup: - Automatic start on boot - Configure kiosk mode - Manage multiple displays

➡️ Scoreboard Autostart Setup

docs.title: 3. Server Architecture
docs.text:
System overview: - Component architecture - Rails application stack - Database design - WebSocket communication - Caching strategies

➡️ Server Architecture Documentation

docs.title: 4. Maintenance & Updates
docs.text:
Regular maintenance: - Apply system updates - Perform Carambus updates - Backup checks - Log rotation - Performance monitoring

Backup & Restore: - Database backups - File backups (uploads, logs) - Restore procedures - Disaster recovery

docs.title: 5. Security
docs.text:
System hardening: - Firewall configuration (ufw) - Fail2ban against brute force - SSL/TLS certificates (Let's Encrypt) - Secure credential management - Access controls

Best practices: - Regular security updates - Enforce strong passwords - Enable 2FA (optional) - Log monitoring - Penetration tests

➡️ Security Best Practices

docs.title: 6. Monitoring & Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Performance monitoring: - CPU/RAM utilization - Database performance - WebSocket connections - Request times - Error rates

Log analysis: - Application logs - Nginx/Apache logs - PostgreSQL logs - Systemd logs

Common problems: - WebSocket connections drop - Slow queries in database - Disk space full - SSL certificate expired

docs.title: 7. Table Reservation & Heating Control
docs.text:
Hardware integration: - Connect heating control - GPIO pins (Raspberry Pi) - Relay modules - Timer switches

➡️ Table Reservation & Heating Control

docs.title: 8. YouTube Live Streaming
docs.text:
Tournament streaming with existing scoreboards: - Uses existing Scoreboard Raspberry Pis - USB webcam per table (~$80) - FFmpeg hardware encoding - Automatic scoreboard overlay - Central management in admin interface

Documentation: - 🚀 Quick Start (5 Steps) - Get your first stream in 5 minutes - 📖 Complete Setup Guide - Hardware, YouTube setup, configuration, troubleshooting - 💻 Developer Architecture - Technical details for developers

Features: - ✅ Table-based streaming (each table independent) - ✅ Live overlays (player names, scores, tournament info) - ✅ Auto-restart on errors - ✅ Health monitoring - ✅ Very cost-effective (~$80 camera per table)

docs.title: Raspberry Pi All-in-One
docs.text:
Hardware requirements: - Raspberry Pi 4 (8GB RAM recommended) or Raspberry Pi 5 - Micro SD card (64 GB, Class 10) - Power supply (USB-C, 3A) - HDMI cable - Optional: Touch display (7" or larger)

Software setup: 1. Download image: Pre-configured Carambus image 2. Flash SD card: With Balena Etcher or Raspberry Pi Imager 3. Initial configuration: WiFi, club name, admin account 4. Done!: System boots in kiosk mode

➡️ Detailed Raspberry Pi Guide

docs.title: Cloud Hosting (VPS)
docs.text:
Provider recommendations: - Hetzner Cloud: 8 EUR/month (CPX21: 3 vCPU, 4 GB RAM) - DigitalOcean: 24 USD/month (4 GB Droplet) - AWS/Azure: From 30 EUR/month (variable costs)

Installation steps: 1. Book and start VPS 2. Install Ubuntu 22.04 LTS 3. Harden base system 4. Install dependencies 5. Deploy Carambus 6. Configure web server 7. Set up SSL 8. Configure systemd service 9. Configure backup 10. Set up monitoring

➡️ Cloud Installation Guide

➡️ On-Premise Installation Guide

docs.title: Problem: Application won't start
docs.text:
Debugging: 

sudo systemctl status carambus
sudo journalctl -u carambus -n 100

Common causes: - Database not reachable - Missing credentials - Port already in use - Missing dependencies

docs.title: Problem: WebSockets not working
docs.text:
Symptoms: Scoreboards don't update in real-time

Checks: 

sudo nginx -t
tail -f log/production.log | grep Cable
redis-cli ping  # if using Redis

docs.title: Problem: Slow performance
docs.text:
Diagnosis: 

htop
sudo -u postgres psql -c "SELECT count(*) FROM pg_stat_activity;"

docs.title: Problem: Disk space full
docs.text:
Solution: 

df -h
sudo journalctl --vacuum-time=7d
rails log:clear

docs.title: 🎯 Scenario Management (Recommended)
docs.text:
Scenario Management - Modern deployment system for various Carambus environments.

Supported Scenarios: - carambus - Main production environment - carambus_location_5101 - Local server instance for location 5101 - carambus_location_2459 - Local server instance for location 2459 - carambus_location_2460 - Local server instance for location 2460

Advantages of Scenario Management: - ✅ Automated deployments - ✅ Consistent configuration - ✅ Integrated SSL management - ✅ Automatic sequence management - ✅ Scalable architecture

docs.title: Raspberry Pi as Client/Display
docs.text: This document describes how to set up a Raspberry Pi as a pure client (display/scoreboard) that connects to an existing Carambus server.

docs.title: Difference to All-in-One
docs.text:
• All-in-One: Raspberry Pi is server AND display
  • Client: Raspberry Pi is only display, connects to external server

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text:
• Raspberry Pi 4 or 5
  • Display (HDMI or touch)
  • Network connection to server

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
1. Install Raspberry Pi OS Lite
  2. Chromium in kiosk mode
  3. Autostart script
# Example autostart
chromium-browser --kiosk --noerrdialogs \
  --disable-infobars --disable-session-crashed-bubble \
  https://carambus-server.local/table_monitors/1

➡️ Details see: Raspberry Pi Client Integration (German only for now)

More information will follow in a future version.

docs.title: Quick Start: Raspberry Pi Scoreboard Installation
docs.text: Complete installation guide from blank SD card to functioning scoreboard in under 30 minutes.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This guide walks you through the complete setup of a Carambus Scoreboard on a Raspberry Pi, including:

  • ✅ Raspberry Pi OS installation
  • ✅ Ansible configuration
  • ✅ Server deployment
  • ✅ Client/Kiosk setup
  • ✅ Automatic browser start

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text:
• Raspberry Pi 4 or 5 (recommended: 4GB+ RAM)
  • MicroSD card (minimum 16GB, recommended 32GB+)
  • Power supply (official Raspberry Pi power adapter recommended)
  • Monitor with HDMI connection
  • Keyboard and mouse (for initial setup)
  • Network connection (Ethernet recommended, WiFi supported)

docs.title: Software
docs.text:
• Computer with SD card reader
  • Raspberry Pi Imager

docs.title: Knowledge Requirements
docs.text:
• Basic command line experience
  • Understanding of SSH connections
  • Basic network configuration knowledge

docs.title: 1.1 Download and Install Raspberry Pi Imager
docs.text: Download from: https://www.raspberrypi.com/software/

docs.title: 1.2 Flash Raspberry Pi OS
docs.text:
1. Insert SD card into computer
  2. Launch Raspberry Pi Imager
  3. Choose Device: Select your Raspberry Pi model
  4. Choose OS:
  5. Raspberry Pi OS (64-bit) - Recommended
  6. Or: Raspberry Pi OS Lite (64-bit) for headless setup
  7. Choose Storage: Select your SD card
  8. Configure Settings (click gear icon ⚙️): 
    Hostname: raspberrypi (or your preferred name)
Username: pi
Password: [your-secure-password]
WiFi: [configure if needed]
Locale: [your timezone and keyboard layout]

✅ Enable SSH
☐ Use password authentication (recommended for initial setup)
  9. Click Write and wait for completion

docs.title: 1.3 First Boot
docs.text:
1. Insert SD card into Raspberry Pi
  2. Connect monitor, keyboard, and network cable
  3. Power on
  4. Wait for boot (first boot may take 2-3 minutes)
  5. Note the IP address (shown on screen or check your router)

Verify SSH Access: 

ssh pi@raspberrypi.local
# or
ssh pi@<IP-ADDRESS>

docs.title: 2.1 Initial Server Setup
docs.text:
Run Ansible playbook to configure the Raspberry Pi as a server:

cd carambus_master
ansible-playbook -i ansible/inventory/production.yml \
  ansible/playbooks/raspberry_pi_server.yml \
  --extra-vars "target_host=raspberrypi.local" \
  --ask-become-pass

This will: - ✅ Update system packages - ✅ Install Ruby, Rails, PostgreSQL, Nginx - ✅ Configure PostgreSQL - ✅ Create www-data user - ✅ Set up directory structures - ✅ Configure firewall

Duration: ~10 minutes (depending on network speed)

docs.title: 3.1 Create Scenario Configuration
docs.text:
If not already exists, create a scenario configuration:

cd carambus_data/scenarios
cp -r carambus_location_template carambus_bcw  # Example name
cd carambus_bcw

Edit config.yml: 

scenario:
  name: carambus_bcw
  description: "Billardclub Wedel"
  location_id: 1
  context: NBV
  region_id: 1
  club_id: 357

environments:
  production:
    webserver_host: 192.168.178.107  # Your Raspberry Pi IP
    ssh_host: 192.168.178.107
    webserver_port: 3131
    ssh_port: 8910
    database_name: carambus_bcw_production
    database_username: www_data
    database_password: [secure-password]

    raspberry_pi_client:
      enabled: true
      ip_address: "192.168.178.107"  # Same as server (all-in-one)
      ssh_user: "www-data"
      ssh_port: 8910
      kiosk_user: "pi"
      local_server_enabled: true
      autostart_enabled: true

docs.title: 3.2 Run Complete Deployment
docs.text:
Use the automated deployment workflow:

cd carambus_master
rake "scenario:deploy_complete[carambus_bcw]"

This comprehensive task will:

  1. Generate Configuration Files
  2. Database configuration
  3. Nginx configuration
  4. Puma service files

  5. Credentials

  6. Create Database Dump

  7. Export development database

  8. Compress for transfer

  9. Deploy to Server

  10. Upload database dump
  11. Restore on production server
  12. Upload configuration files

  13. Deploy application code (via Capistrano)

  14. Setup Raspberry Pi Client

  15. Install required packages (Chromium, wmctrl, xdotool)
  16. Configure kiosk user

  17. Create systemd service

  18. Deploy Client Configuration

  19. Upload scoreboard URL
  20. Install autostart script

  21. Enable and start kiosk service

  22. Test Everything

  23. Verify SSH connection
  24. Check systemd service
  25. Confirm browser is running

Duration: ~10 minutes

Expected Output: 

🎉 COMPLETE WORKFLOW SUCCESSFUL!
================================
Scenario 'carambus_bcw' is now fully deployed and operational

Access Information:
  - Web Interface: http://192.168.178.107:3131
  - SSH Access: ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107

Management Commands:
  - Restart Browser: rake scenario:restart_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_bcw]
  - Test Client: rake scenario:test_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_bcw]
  - Check Service: ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo systemctl status scoreboard-kiosk'

docs.title: 4.1 Check Web Interface
docs.text:
Open browser on another computer: 

http://192.168.178.107:3131

docs.title: 4.2 Verify Scoreboard Display
docs.text: The Raspberry Pi monitor should show: - ✅ Fullscreen Chromium browser - ✅ Carambus Scoreboard welcome screen - ✅ No desktop visible (kiosk mode)

docs.title: 4.3 Test from Command Line
docs.text:
# Check service status
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo systemctl status scoreboard-kiosk'

# Check browser process
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'pgrep -fa chromium'

# View logs
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'tail -50 /tmp/chromium-kiosk.log'

docs.title: Browser Not Starting
docs.text:
Check logs: 

ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo journalctl -u scoreboard-kiosk.service -n 50'
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'cat /tmp/chromium-kiosk.log'

Restart service: 

rake "scenario:restart_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_bcw]"

docs.title: Web Interface Not Accessible
docs.text:
Check Puma service: 

ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo systemctl status puma-carambus_bcw'

Check Nginx: 

ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo systemctl status nginx'

Check database connection: 

ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'cd /var/www/carambus_bcw/current && RAILS_ENV=production bundle exec rails runner "puts Region.count"'

docs.title: Permission Errors
docs.text:
If you see permission errors for Chromium profile directory: 

ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo rm -rf /tmp/chromium-scoreboard*'
rake "scenario:restart_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_bcw]"

docs.title: Daily Operations
docs.text:
Restart Scoreboard Browser: 

rake "scenario:restart_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_bcw]"

Restart Rails Application: 

ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo systemctl restart puma-carambus_bcw'

View Application Logs: 

ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'tail -f /var/www/carambus_bcw/shared/log/production.log'

Reboot Raspberry Pi: 

ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo reboot'

docs.title: Updates and Maintenance
docs.text:
Update Application Code: 

cd carambus_master
rake "scenario:deploy[carambus_bcw,production]"

Update System Packages: 

ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y'

Backup Database: 

rake "scenario:create_database_dump[carambus_bcw,production]"

Restore Database: 

rake "scenario:restore_database_dump[carambus_bcw,production]"

docs.title: Custom Port Configuration
docs.text:
Edit config.yml to change ports: 

environments:
  production:
    webserver_port: 3131  # Change to your preferred port
    ssh_port: 8910        # Change SSH port if needed

docs.title: Multiple Locations
docs.text:
For multiple tables/locations in one club: 

scenario:
  location_id: 1  # First table

# Create separate scenarios for each table:
# - carambus_bcw_table1
# - carambus_bcw_table2
# - carambus_bcw_table3

docs.title: Headless Setup (No Monitor)
docs.text:
For remote-only access without kiosk mode: 

raspberry_pi_client:
  enabled: false  # Disable kiosk mode

docs.title: Success Checklist
docs.text:
After completing all steps, verify:

  • Raspberry Pi boots successfully
  • SSH access works
  • Web interface accessible from network
  • PostgreSQL database running
  • Rails application responding
  • Nginx proxy working
  • Scoreboard displays on monitor
  • Browser starts automatically after reboot
  • Touch input working (if touch display)
  • No error messages in logs

docs.title: Support
docs.text:
If you encounter issues:

  1. Review system logs
  2. Verify network configuration
  3. Check GitHub issues: https://github.com/GernotUllrich/carambus/issues

docs.title: Credits
docs.text:
This installation process has been streamlined through extensive testing and automation. The complete workflow - from blank SD card to functioning scoreboard - typically takes 25-30 minutes.

Key Technologies: - Raspberry Pi OS (Debian) - Ruby on Rails 7.2 - PostgreSQL 15 - Nginx - Puma - Chromium (Kiosk Mode) - Ansible - Capistrano

Last Updated: October 2025 Tested On: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (4GB), Raspberry Pi 5 (8GB) OS Version: Raspberry Pi OS (Bookworm/Trixie, 64-bit)

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Raspberry Pi Client System has been integrated into the Scenario Management System to enable automatic deployment and management of kiosk browsers for Carambus scoreboards.

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
1. Scenario Configuration: Raspberry Pi Client settings in config.yml
  2. Rake Tasks: Automated setup, deployment, and management tasks
  3. Systemd Service: Kiosk mode as system service
  4. Autostart Script: Intelligent browser start script with display management

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
Scenario Config → Rake Task → SSH → Raspberry Pi
     ↓              ↓         ↓         ↓
  config.yml → deploy_raspberry_pi_client → SSH Commands → Kiosk Browser

docs.title: Raspberry Pi Client Configuration
docs.text:
Each scenario can contain Raspberry Pi Client settings:

environments:
  production:
    # ... other configurations ...
    raspberry_pi_client:
      enabled: true
      ip_address: "192.168.178.92"  # Raspberry Pi IP
      ssh_user: "pi"
      ssh_password: "raspberry"
      kiosk_user: "pi"
      local_server_enabled: true  # Does this location host a local server?
      local_server_port: 8910
      autostart_enabled: true
      browser_restart_command: "sudo systemctl restart scoreboard-kiosk"

docs.title: Configuration Options
docs.text: Option Description Default enabled Enables Raspberry Pi Client for this scenario false ip_address IP address of the Raspberry Pi - ssh_user SSH username pi ssh_password SSH password raspberry kiosk_user User for kiosk mode pi local_server_enabled Does this location host a local server? false local_server_port Port of the local server 8910 autostart_enabled Automatic start on boot true browser_restart_command Command to restart the browser sudo systemctl restart scoreboard-kiosk

docs.title: 1. Setup Raspberry Pi Client
docs.text:
rake scenario:setup_raspberry_pi_client[scenario_name]

Purpose: Initial setup of the Raspberry Pi for kiosk mode

Steps: 1. Tests SSH connection 2. Installs required packages (chromium-browser, wmctrl, xdotool) 3. Creates kiosk user (if required) 4. Sets up autostart configuration 5. Creates systemd service

Example: 

rake scenario:setup_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_location_2459]

docs.title: 2. Deploy Raspberry Pi Client
docs.text:
rake scenario:deploy_raspberry_pi_client[scenario_name]

Purpose: Deployment of kiosk configuration to the Raspberry Pi

Steps: 1. Generates scoreboard URL based on location_id (MD5 hash) 2. Uploads scoreboard URL to Raspberry Pi 3. Uploads and installs autostart script 4. Enables and starts systemd service

Example: 

rake scenario:deploy_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_location_2459]

docs.title: 3. Restart Raspberry Pi Client
docs.text:
rake scenario:restart_raspberry_pi_client[scenario_name]

Purpose: Restart kiosk browser via SSH

Functionality: - Executes the configured restart command - Enables quick restart without Raspberry Pi reboot - Saves time for tests and updates

Example: 

rake scenario:restart_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_location_2459]

docs.title: 4. Test Raspberry Pi Client
docs.text:
rake scenario:test_raspberry_pi_client[scenario_name]

Purpose: Test of Raspberry Pi Client functionality

Tests: 1. SSH connection 2. Systemd service status 3. Scoreboard URL file 4. Browser process

Example: 

rake scenario:test_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_location_2459]

docs.title: Automatic URL Creation
docs.text:
The system automatically generates the correct scoreboard URL:

location_id = scenario_config['scenario']['location_id']
location_md5 = Digest::MD5.hexdigest(location_id.to_s)
scoreboard_url = "http://#{webserver_host}:#{webserver_port}/locations/#{location_md5}?sb_state=welcome"

docs.title: Example
docs.text: For location_id: 2459: - MD5 hash: a1b2c3d4e5f6... - URL: http://192.168.178.107:81/locations/a1b2c3d4e5f6...?sb_state=welcome

docs.title: Service Definition
docs.text:
[Unit]
Description=Carambus Scoreboard Kiosk
After=graphical.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=pi
Environment=DISPLAY=:0
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/autostart-scoreboard.sh
Restart=always
RestartSec=10

[Install]
WantedBy=graphical.target

docs.title: Service Management
docs.text:
# Enable service
sudo systemctl enable scoreboard-kiosk

# Start service
sudo systemctl start scoreboard-kiosk

# Restart service
sudo systemctl restart scoreboard-kiosk

# Check service status
sudo systemctl status scoreboard-kiosk

docs.title: Intelligent Browser Management
docs.text:
The generated autostart script provides:

  1. Display Management: Waits for display readiness
  2. Panel Hiding: Hides desktop panels for fullscreen mode
  3. Browser Optimization: Special Chromium flags for kiosk mode
  4. Error Handling: Robust handling of display issues

docs.title: Script Features
docs.text:
#!/bin/bash
# Carambus Scoreboard Autostart Script

# Set display environment
export DISPLAY=:0

# Wait for display to be ready
sleep 5

# Hide panel
wmctrl -r "panel" -b add,hidden 2>/dev/null || true
wmctrl -r "lxpanel" -b add,hidden 2>/dev/null || true

# Get scoreboard URL
SCOREBOARD_URL=$(cat /etc/scoreboard_url)

# Start browser in fullscreen
/usr/bin/chromium-browser \
  --start-fullscreen \
  --disable-restore-session-state \
  --user-data-dir=/tmp/chromium-scoreboard \
  --disable-features=VizDisplayCompositor \
  --disable-dev-shm-usage \
  --app="$SCOREBOARD_URL" \
  >/dev/null 2>&1 &

# Ensure fullscreen
sleep 5
wmctrl -r "Chromium" -b add,fullscreen 2>/dev/null || true

docs.title: SSH Key-based Authentication (Recommended)
docs.text:
The system supports both SSH key and password authentication:

# SSH key authentication (passwordless)
ssh -p 8910 -o ConnectTimeout=10 -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no www-data@192.168.178.107 'command'

# Password authentication (if required)
sshpass -p 'password' ssh -p 8910 -o ConnectTimeout=10 -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no user@ip 'command'

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Prefer SSH keys: Passwordless SSH is more secure and convenient
  • www-data user: Specially configured for server management
  • Port 8910: Non-standard port for additional security
  • Firewall: Restrict SSH access to trusted IPs

docs.title: Complete Setup Workflow
docs.text:
# 1. Prepare scenario for development
rake scenario:prepare_development[carambus_location_2459,development]

# 2. Prepare scenario for deployment
rake scenario:prepare_deploy[carambus_location_2459]

# 3. Server deployment
rake scenario:deploy[carambus_location_2459]

# 4. Raspberry Pi Client setup
rake scenario:setup_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_location_2459]

# 5. Raspberry Pi Client deployment
rake scenario:deploy_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_location_2459]

# 6. Test
rake scenario:test_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_location_2459]

docs.title: Quick Browser Restart
docs.text:
# Restart browser (without Raspberry Pi reboot)
rake scenario:restart_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_location_2459]

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
# Check client status
rake scenario:test_raspberry_pi_client[carambus_location_2459]

# Check service status on Raspberry Pi
ssh pi@192.168.178.92 "sudo systemctl status scoreboard-kiosk"

# Check browser processes
ssh pi@192.168.178.92 "pgrep chromium-browser"

docs.title: Location with Local Server (e.g. carambus_location_2459)
docs.text:
• Local Server: Runs on port 8910
  • SSH Access: Via www-data user
  • Scoreboard URL: Points to local server

docs.title: Location without Local Server (e.g. carambus_location_2460)
docs.text:
• No Local Server: Connects to API server
  • SSH Access: Via standard pi user
  • Scoreboard URL: Points to API server

docs.title: Common Problems
docs.text:
1. SSH Connection Failed
  2. Check IP address and network connection
  3. Check SSH service status on Raspberry Pi

  4. Check firewall settings

  5. Browser Won't Start

  6. Check display environment (echo $DISPLAY)
  7. Check Chromium installation

  8. Check scoreboard URL file

  9. Fullscreen Mode Not Working

  10. Check wmctrl installation
  11. Check desktop environment (LXDE)

  12. Check display resolution

  13. Service Won't Start

  14. Check systemd service definition
  15. Check user permissions
  16. Check logs: sudo journalctl -u scoreboard-kiosk

docs.title: Debug Commands
docs.text:
# Show service logs
sudo journalctl -u scoreboard-kiosk -f

# Show browser processes
ps aux | grep chromium

# Check display environment
echo $DISPLAY
xrandr

# Check scoreboard URL
cat /etc/scoreboard_url

docs.title: Production Environment
docs.text:
1. Use SSH keys: Replace password authentication
  2. Configure firewall: Restrict SSH access
  3. Regular updates: Keep Raspberry Pi OS up to date
  4. Monitoring: Monitor service status

docs.title: Network Security
docs.text:
1. VLAN segmentation: Isolate kiosk network
  2. VPN access: For remote management
  3. Certificate validation: For HTTPS connections

docs.title: Planned Features
docs.text:
1. SSH key authentication: Replace password authentication
  2. Automatic updates: OTA updates for Raspberry Pi
  3. Monitoring integration: Health checks and alerting
  4. Multi-display support: Support for multiple monitors
  5. Backup system: Automatic configuration backups

docs.title: Performance Optimizations
docs.text:
1. Browser caching: Optimized cache settings
  2. Display optimization: Automatic resolution adjustment
  3. Startup optimization: Faster boot times

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The Raspberry Pi Client System provides:

✅ Complete integration into the Scenario Management System ✅ Automated deployment of kiosk browsers ✅ SSH-based management for remote operations ✅ Intelligent scoreboard URL generation based on location_id ✅ Robust browser management with systemd service ✅ Flexible configuration for different location types ✅ Comprehensive test and debug tools

The system enables efficient management of Raspberry Pi-based kiosk clients and integration into the existing Carambus infrastructure.

docs.title: 2025-10-17: Compatibility with Debian Trixie and Utility Scripts
docs.text:
Changes:

  1. Chromium Package Name Updated (Commit: ca4c665)
  2. Newer Raspberry Pi OS versions (Debian Trixie) use chromium instead of chromium-browser
  3. bin/setup-raspi-table-client.sh adapted:
    • Installation: chromium instead of chromium-browser
    • Executable: /usr/bin/chromium instead of /usr/bin/chromium-browser

  4. Fixes installation error: "Package chromium-browser is not available"

  5. New Utility Scripts Added (Commit: c304d18)

  6. bin/check-database-states.sh: Comprehensive analysis tool

    • Compares database states between Local, Production and API Server
    • Checks version IDs, table_locals, tournament_locals
    • Warns about unbumped IDs (< 50,000,000)
    • Shows ID ranges and local data
    • Usage: ./bin/check-database-states.sh <scenario_name>

  7. bin/puma-wrapper.sh: Systemd service wrapper

    • Initializes rbenv correctly for Puma service
    • Changes to the correct deployment directory
    • Usage: puma-wrapper.sh <basename> or via PUMA_BASENAME environment variable

  8. Scoreboard Menu Integration Completed

  9. Branch scorebord_menu successfully integrated into master
  10. NetworkManager support in setup script present
  11. Automatic detection of dhcpcd vs. NetworkManager

Compatibility:

  • ✅ Raspberry Pi OS (Debian Bullseye) - chromium-browser fallback available
  • ✅ Raspberry Pi OS (Debian Trixie/Bookworm) - Primary support
  • ✅ dhcpcd-based network configuration
  • ✅ NetworkManager-based configuration

Deployment Notes:

When setting up on new Raspberry Pi with Debian Trixie: 

sh bin/setup-raspi-table-client.sh carambus_bcw <current_ip> \
  <ssid> <password> <static_ip> <table_number> [ssh_port] [ssh_user] [server_ip]

The script automatically detects: - The correct Chromium package name - The network management system being used (dhcpcd/NetworkManager) - Configures WLAN and static IP accordingly

docs.title: Raspberry Pi Management Scripts
docs.text: This documentation describes all available scripts for managing Raspberry Pi clients in the Carambus system.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Raspberry Pi scripts are located in carambus_master/bin/ and cover the following areas: - Setup & Installation: Complete setup of new RasPi clients - Testing & Debugging: Network scans and functionality checks - Scoreboard Management: Kiosk mode and browser control - Utilities: SSH setup, SD card preparation

docs.title: setup-raspberry-pi.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Complete setup of a Raspberry Pi as Carambus client

Usage: 

cd carambus_master
./bin/setup-raspberry-pi.sh <scenario_name>

What it does: 1. ✅ Installs required packages (chromium, wmctrl, xdotool) 2. ✅ Creates kiosk user 3. ✅ Sets up systemd service 4. ✅ Configures autostart for scoreboard 5. ✅ Starts kiosk mode

Prerequisites: - SSH access to Raspberry Pi - Scenario configuration in carambus_data/scenarios/<scenario_name>/config.yml - Raspberry Pi already running with OS

Documented in: Client-Only Installation

Example: 

./bin/setup-raspberry-pi.sh carambus_location_5101
# Sets up RasPi for Location 5101

docs.title: install-client-only.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Installation of a pure client system (without local server)

Usage: 

./bin/install-client-only.sh <scenario_name>

What it does: 1. ✅ Configures browser kiosk mode 2. ✅ Sets up autostart for scoreboard 3. ✅ Connects to remote server (no local Puma) 4. ✅ Optimized for minimal resource usage

Use Case: Tablet or RasPi that only serves as display

Documented in: Client-Only Installation

docs.title: install-scoreboard-client.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Installs scoreboard client software

Usage: 

./bin/install-scoreboard-client.sh

What it does: - Installs client dependencies - Configures browser for fullscreen - Sets up autostart

docs.title: setup-phillips-table-ssh.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Set up SSH access for Phillips Table

Usage: 

./bin/setup-phillips-table-ssh.sh

What it does: 1. ✅ Generates SSH keys 2. ✅ Copies public key to RasPi 3. ✅ Configures passwordless SSH 4. ✅ Tests connection

Prerequisites: - Initial password for RasPi known - Network access to RasPi

Example: 

./bin/setup-phillips-table-ssh.sh
# Interactive: Asks for IP, port, password

docs.title: find-raspberry-pi.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Finds Raspberry Pis in local network

Usage: 

./bin/find-raspberry-pi.sh [subnet]

What it does: - Scans network for RasPi hosts - Shows IP addresses and hostnames - Checks SSH availability

Examples: 

# Standard scan in local network
./bin/find-raspberry-pi.sh

# Scan specific subnet
./bin/find-raspberry-pi.sh 192.168.178.0/24

Output Example: 

Scanning for Raspberry Pis in 192.168.178.0/24...
Found: 192.168.178.107 (raspberrypi.local)
  SSH: Available (Port 8910)
  Service: puma-carambus_location_5101

docs.title: test-raspberry-pi.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Comprehensive tests for RasPi functionality

Usage: 

./bin/test-raspberry-pi.sh <scenario_name>

What is tested: 1. ✅ SSH connection 2. ✅ Puma service status 3. ✅ Nginx configuration 4. ✅ Database connectivity 5. ✅ Scoreboard accessibility 6. ✅ Browser kiosk mode

Example: 

./bin/test-raspberry-pi.sh carambus_location_5101
# Runs all tests and shows results

docs.title: test-raspberry-pi-restart.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Tests RasPi restart functionality

Usage: 

./bin/test-raspberry-pi-restart.sh <scenario_name>

What is tested: - Restart command works - Services start correctly after reboot - Scoreboard starts automatically

docs.title: start-scoreboard.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Starts scoreboard in kiosk mode

Usage: 

# On the RasPi:
./bin/start-scoreboard.sh [url]

# Remote from development machine:
ssh pi@raspberrypi.local '/path/to/start-scoreboard.sh'

What it does: 1. ✅ Starts Chromium in fullscreen mode 2. ✅ Opens scoreboard URL 3. ✅ Hides panel/taskbar 4. ✅ Disables screensaver

Prerequisites: - X11 display available - Chromium installed

docs.title: autostart-scoreboard.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Scoreboard autostart configuration

Usage: 

./bin/autostart-scoreboard.sh <scenario_name>

What it does: - Creates systemd service - Configures autostart on boot - Waits for Puma server - Starts browser automatically

Documented in: Scoreboard Autostart Setup

docs.title: restart-scoreboard.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Restart scoreboard browser

Usage: 

# Locally on RasPi:
./bin/restart-scoreboard.sh

# Remote via SSH:
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 './bin/restart-scoreboard.sh'

What it does: 1. ✅ Terminates running Chromium processes 2. ✅ Cleans cache 3. ✅ Starts browser with scoreboard URL

Use Cases: - Browser hangs or is slow - After software update - After change of scoreboard URL

docs.title: exit-scoreboard.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Exits scoreboard kiosk mode cleanly

Usage: 

./bin/exit-scoreboard.sh

What it does: - Terminates Chromium processes - Shows panel/taskbar again - Cleans temporary files

docs.title: cleanup-chromium.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Cleans Chromium cache and temporary files

Usage: 

./bin/cleanup-chromium.sh

What it does: - Deletes browser cache - Removes cookies - Cleans downloads - Deletes crash reports

Use Cases: - Browser becomes slow - Too little disk space - After long runtime

docs.title: prepare-sd-card.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Prepares SD card for RasPi installation

Usage: 

./bin/prepare-sd-card.sh [device]

What it does: 1. ⚠️ Formats SD card (WARNING: All data will be deleted!) 2. ✅ Installs Raspberry Pi OS 3. ✅ Configures SSH 4. ✅ Configures WLAN (optional) 5. ✅ Creates basic configuration

Prerequisites: - SD card inserted - Raspberry Pi OS image downloaded - Admin rights (sudo)

Example: 

# List available devices
diskutil list

# Prepare SD card
sudo ./bin/prepare-sd-card.sh /dev/disk2

⚠️ WARNING: Check device carefully! Wrong device leads to data loss.

docs.title: quick-start-raspberry-pi.sh ⚠️
docs.text: Status: Obsolete (replaced by setup-raspberry-pi.sh)

docs.title: auto-setup-raspberry-pi.sh ⚠️
docs.text: Status: Obsolete (replaced by setup-raspberry-pi.sh)

docs.title: start_scoreboard ⚠️
docs.text: Status: Obsolete (replaced by start-scoreboard.sh)

docs.title: start_scoreboard_delayed ⚠️
docs.text: Status: Obsolete (replaced by autostart-scoreboard.sh)

docs.title: Setting up a New Raspberry Pi from Scratch
docs.text:
# 1. Prepare SD card
sudo ./bin/prepare-sd-card.sh /dev/disk2

# 2. Boot RasPi and find it in network
./bin/find-raspberry-pi.sh

# 3. Set up SSH access
./bin/setup-phillips-table-ssh.sh
# IP: 192.168.178.107
# Port: 22
# Password: [initial password]

# 4. Complete installation
./bin/setup-raspberry-pi.sh carambus_location_5101

# 5. Test
./bin/test-raspberry-pi.sh carambus_location_5101

docs.title: Fixing Browser Problems
docs.text:
# 1. Clean Chromium cache
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 './bin/cleanup-chromium.sh'

# 2. Restart browser
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 './bin/restart-scoreboard.sh'

# 3. If still problems: Restart service
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo systemctl restart scoreboard-kiosk'

docs.title: Deploy Scenario Update to RasPi
docs.text:
# 1. Deployment from development machine
cd carambus_master
./bin/deploy-scenario.sh carambus_location_5101

# 2. Restart browser on RasPi (to load new assets)
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 './bin/restart-scoreboard.sh'

# 3. Test
./bin/test-raspberry-pi.sh carambus_location_5101

docs.title: SSH Connection Fails
docs.text:
# Problem: "Connection refused"
# Solution: Enable SSH manually on RasPi
# 1. Connect monitor + keyboard to RasPi
# 2. sudo raspi-config
# 3. Interface Options → SSH → Enable

# Problem: "Permission denied (publickey)"
# Solution: Set up SSH keys again
./bin/setup-phillips-table-ssh.sh

docs.title: Browser Won't Start
docs.text:
# Problem: "Display not available"
# Solution: Check X11 display
ssh -p 8910 www-data@raspberrypi 'echo $DISPLAY'
# Should be ":0"

# If not set:
ssh -p 8910 www-data@raspberrypi 'export DISPLAY=:0 && ./bin/start-scoreboard.sh'

docs.title: Scoreboard Shows Old Version
docs.text:
# Clean cache and restart browser
ssh -p 8910 www-data@raspberrypi './bin/cleanup-chromium.sh && ./bin/restart-scoreboard.sh'

# If that doesn't help: Hard reload in browser
# Ctrl+Shift+R or systemctl restart

docs.title: RasPi Setup
docs.text:
1. ✅ Always use find-raspberry-pi.sh first to determine IP
  2. ✅ Set up SSH keys (passwordless) for automation
  3. ✅ Restart browser after each deployment
  4. ✅ Regularly clean Chromium cache

docs.title: Network
docs.text:
1. ✅ Use static IP for production RasPis
  2. ✅ Change SSH port (standard: 8910 instead of 22)
  3. ✅ Configure firewall on RasPi

docs.title: Maintenance
docs.text:
1. ✅ Weekly: Run cleanup-chromium.sh
  2. ✅ Monthly: OS updates via apt update && apt upgrade
  3. ✅ When problems arise: First browser restart, then service restart, then reboot

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Client-Only Installation - Detailed installation guide
  • Scoreboard Autostart Setup - Autostart configuration
  • Deployment Workflow - Complete deployment process
  • Scenario Management - Scenario system overview

docs.title: Step 1: Install required tools
docs.text:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install wmctrl

docs.title: Step 2: Configure the scoreboard URL
docs.text:
Edit the config file to set your scoreboard URL: 

nano config/scoreboard_url

The default URL is: 

http://localhost:3000/locations/1/scoreboard_reservations

Change it to your actual scoreboard URL if needed.

docs.title: Step 3: Make scripts executable
docs.text:
chmod +x bin/start-scoreboard.sh
chmod +x bin/exit-scoreboard.sh
chmod +x bin/restart-scoreboard.sh

docs.title: Step 4: Test the startup script manually
docs.text: ./bin/start-scoreboard.sh

docs.title: Step 5: If it works, add to autostart
docs.text:
nano ~/.config/lxsession/LXDE-pi/autostart

Add this line at the end (adjust path to your Rails app location): 

@/path/to/your/rails/app/bin/autostart-scoreboard.sh

For example, if your Rails app is in /var/www/carambus/current: 

@/var/www/carambus/current/bin/autostart-scoreboard.sh

Note: Use the autostart-scoreboard.sh wrapper script instead of start-scoreboard.sh directly for better autostart compatibility.

docs.title: Step 6: Reboot to test
docs.text: sudo reboot

docs.title: Configure Keyboard Shortcuts (Optional)
docs.text:
nano ~/.config/labwc/rc.xml

Add inside <keyboard> section (adjust paths to your Rails app location): 

<keybind key="F12">
  <action name="Execute">
    <command>/path/to/your/rails/app/bin/exit-scoreboard.sh</command>
  </action>
</keybind>

<keybind key="F11">
  <action name="Execute">
    <command>/path/to/your/rails/app/bin/restart-scoreboard.sh</command>
  </action>
</keybind>

For example, if your Rails app is in /home/pi/carambus_gernot: 

<keybind key="F12">
  <action name="Execute">
    <command>/home/pi/carambus_gernot/bin/exit-scoreboard.sh</command>
  </action>
</keybind>

<keybind key="F11">
  <action name="Execute">
    <command>/home/pi/carambus_gernot/bin/restart-scoreboard.sh</command>
  </action>
</keybind>

docs.title: Alternative: Systemd User Service (Recommended)
docs.text: If window manager autostart doesn't work, use systemd user service instead:

docs.title: Step 1: Create systemd user service for www-data user
docs.text:
# Switch to www-data user
sudo -u www-data bash

# Create systemd user directory in www-data's home
mkdir -p /var/www/.config/systemd/user
nano /var/www/.config/systemd/user/scoreboard.service

Add this content: 

[Unit]
Description=Scoreboard Autostart
After=graphical-session.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
Environment=DISPLAY=:0
ExecStart=/var/www/carambus/current/bin/autostart-scoreboard.sh
RemainAfterExit=yes

[Install]
WantedBy=graphical-session.target

docs.title: Step 2: Enable and start the service as www-data
docs.text:
# Still as www-data user
systemctl --user enable scoreboard.service
systemctl --user start scoreboard.service

docs.title: Step 3: Check service status
docs.text: systemctl --user status scoreboard.service

docs.title: Step 4: Test autostart
docs.text: sudo reboot

docs.title: Step 5: Check if it worked
docs.text:
# As www-data user
cat /tmp/scoreboard-autostart.log
systemctl --user status scoreboard.service

docs.title: Remote management as www-data user
docs.text:
# SSH as www-data user
ssh www-data@raspberrypi

# Then use systemctl commands
systemctl --user restart scoreboard.service
systemctl --user status scoreboard.service
systemctl --user stop scoreboard.service
systemctl --user start scoreboard.service

# View logs
cat /tmp/scoreboard-autostart.log
journalctl --user -u scoreboard.service

docs.title: Troubleshooting systemd service
docs.text:
# Check service logs (as www-data user)
journalctl --user -u scoreboard.service

# Restart service
systemctl --user restart scoreboard.service

# Disable service
systemctl --user disable scoreboard.service

docs.title: Alternative: System-wide Service (Recommended for www-data)
docs.text: Since www-data user doesn't have display access, use a system-wide service instead:

docs.title: Step 1: Create system-wide service
docs.text:
sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/scoreboard.service

Add this content: 

[Unit]
Description=Scoreboard Autostart
After=graphical-session.target

[Service]
Type=oneshot
User=pj
Environment=DISPLAY=:0
Environment=XAUTHORITY=/home/pj/.Xauthority
Environment=HOME=/home/pj
ExecStartPre=/bin/bash -c "sleep 15"
ExecStart=/var/www/carambus/current/bin/autostart-scoreboard.sh
RemainAfterExit=yes

[Install]
WantedBy=graphical-session.target

docs.title: Step 2: Enable and start the service
docs.text:
sudo systemctl enable scoreboard.service
sudo systemctl start scoreboard.service

docs.title: Step 3: Check service status
docs.text: sudo systemctl status scoreboard.service

docs.title: Step 4: Remote management as www-data
docs.text:
# SSH as www-data user
ssh www-data@raspberrypi

# Then use systemctl commands (with sudo)
sudo systemctl restart scoreboard.service
sudo systemctl status scoreboard.service
sudo systemctl stop scoreboard.service
sudo systemctl start scoreboard.service

# View logs
cat /tmp/scoreboard-autostart.log
sudo journalctl -u scoreboard.service

docs.title: Step 5: Test autostart
docs.text:
sudo reboot

This approach runs the service as the pi user (who has display access) but can be controlled by the www-data user via sudo.

docs.title: Troubleshooting system-wide service
docs.text:
# Check service status (as pi user or with sudo)
sudo systemctl status scoreboard.service

# Check detailed logs (as pi user or with sudo)
sudo journalctl -u scoreboard.service -f

# Check if the script works manually (as pi user)
sudo -u pi /var/www/carambus/current/bin/autostart-scoreboard.sh

# Check if www-data can run sudo commands
sudo -l

# Add www-data to sudoers if needed
sudo visudo
# Add this line: www-data ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/systemctl

docs.title: Remote Management
docs.text: The systemd user service enables powerful remote management capabilities:

docs.title: Remote commands
docs.text:
# Restart scoreboard remotely
ssh pi@raspberrypi 'systemctl --user restart scoreboard.service'

# Check status remotely
ssh pi@raspberrypi 'systemctl --user status scoreboard.service'

# View logs remotely
ssh pi@raspberrypi 'cat /tmp/scoreboard-autostart.log'

# Stop scoreboard for maintenance
ssh pi@raspberrypi 'systemctl --user stop scoreboard.service'

# Start scoreboard
ssh pi@raspberrypi 'systemctl --user start scoreboard.service'

docs.title: Exit Methods
docs.text: The scoreboard runs in fullscreen mode, and there are several ways to exit:

docs.title: Primary Exit Methods
docs.text:
1. Physical Keyboard: ALT+F4 or ESC (most reliable)
  2. Remote SSH: systemctl --user restart scoreboard.service

docs.title: Fallback Exit Method
docs.text:
1. "Try Resize Window" Button:
  2. Click the refresh/resize button in the top menu
  3. This refreshes the Chromium window state
  4. Restores the circled X button in the top-right corner
  5. Click the X to exit to desktop

docs.title: Why This Works
docs.text:
• The "Try Resize Window" function refreshes the Chromium window state
  • This restores window controls that get hidden during scoreboard interactions
  • Provides a reliable mouse/touch-based exit method
  • Works when other exit methods fail

docs.title: Button Location
docs.text:
• Table Monitors: Top-right refresh button
  • Scoreboard Pages: Top-right refresh button
  • All scoreboard interfaces: Consistent exit method

This gives users a reliable way to exit the scoreboard even without a keyboard!

docs.title: Check if wmctrl is installed
docs.text: which wmctrl

docs.title: Check if scripts are executable
docs.text:
ls -la bin/start-scoreboard.sh
ls -la bin/exit-scoreboard.sh
ls -la bin/restart-scoreboard.sh

docs.title: Check the scoreboard URL configuration
docs.text: cat config/scoreboard_url

docs.title: Check autostart file
docs.text: cat ~/.config/lxsession/LXDE-pi/autostart

docs.title: Check system logs
docs.text: journalctl -u lxsession

docs.title: Test panel hiding manually
docs.text:
wmctrl -l
wmctrl -r "panel" -b add,hidden
wmctrl -r "panel" -b remove,hidden

docs.title: Check if browser starts correctly
docs.text: ps aux | grep chromium

docs.title: Test scripts manually
docs.text:
# Test startup
./bin/start-scoreboard.sh

# Test exit (in another terminal)
./bin/exit-scoreboard.sh

# Test restart
./bin/restart-scoreboard.sh

docs.title: Check log files
docs.text:
# Debug test log (from test-startup.sh)
cat /tmp/scoreboard-debug.log

# Autostart wrapper log (from autostart-scoreboard.sh)
cat /tmp/scoreboard-autostart.log

# Chromium output (if you ran with nohup)
cat nohup.out

# LXDE autostart logs
tail -f ~/.cache/lxsession/LXDE-pi/run.log

# System logs
journalctl -u lxsession

docs.title: Troubleshooting for /var/www/carambus/current setup
docs.text: If your Rails app is in /var/www/carambus/current and runs as www-data:

docs.title: 1. Check script permissions
docs.text: sudo ls -la /var/www/carambus/current/bin/start-scoreboard.sh

docs.title: 2. Make scripts executable for all users
docs.text:
sudo chmod +x /var/www/carambus/current/bin/start-scoreboard.sh
sudo chmod +x /var/www/carambus/current/bin/exit-scoreboard.sh
sudo chmod +x /var/www/carambus/current/bin/restart-scoreboard.sh

docs.title: 3. Check if the autostart user can read the config file
docs.text:
sudo ls -la /var/www/carambus/current/config/scoreboard_url
sudo chmod 644 /var/www/carambus/current/config/scoreboard_url

docs.title: 4. Test the script manually as the autostart user
docs.text: /var/www/carambus/current/bin/start-scoreboard.sh

docs.title: 5. Check LXDE autostart logs
docs.text: tail -f ~/.cache/lxsession/LXDE-pi/run.log

docs.title: 6. Alternative: Use absolute paths in autostart
docs.text:
Edit the autostart file: 

nano ~/.config/lxsession/LXDE-pi/autostart

Make sure it contains: 

@/var/www/carambus/current/bin/start-scoreboard.sh

docs.title: 7. Check if wmctrl is available for the autostart user
docs.text:
which wmctrl
sudo apt install wmctrl  # if not found

docs.title: 8. Test with debug output
docs.text:
Create a test script to see what's happening: 

sudo nano /var/www/carambus/current/bin/test-startup.sh

Content: 

#!/bin/bash
echo "Starting test at $(date)" >> /tmp/scoreboard-debug.log
echo "Current user: $(whoami)" >> /tmp/scoreboard-debug.log
echo "Current directory: $(pwd)" >> /tmp/scoreboard-debug.log
echo "wmctrl available: $(which wmctrl)" >> /tmp/scoreboard-debug.log
echo "Config file content: $(cat /var/www/carambus/current/config/scoreboard_url)" >> /tmp/scoreboard-debug.log
/var/www/carambus/current/bin/start-scoreboard.sh
echo "Test completed at $(date)" >> /tmp/scoreboard-debug.log

Make executable: 

sudo chmod +x /var/www/carambus/current/bin/test-startup.sh

Test it: 

/var/www/carambus/current/bin/test-startup.sh

Check the debug log: 

cat /tmp/scoreboard-debug.log

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Boot → Panel hidden, scoreboard starts in fullscreen
  2. F12 → Exit to desktop (panel visible)
  3. F11 → Restart scoreboard (panel hidden again)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The scripts are now in your Rails app's bin/ directory and tracked by git
  • The scoreboard URL is configurable via config/scoreboard_url
  • If the panel doesn't hide, try different panel names: panel, lxpanel, lxpanel-pi
  • The 2>/dev/null || true prevents errors if the window doesn't exist
  • Make sure your Rails server is running before testing
  • Update the paths in autostart and keyboard shortcuts to match your actual Rails app location

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Carambus uses a distributed architecture with a central API Server and multiple Local Servers. This design enables efficient data management and optimal performance for local venues.

docs.title: API Server (Central Server)
docs.text:
The API Server is the central data source for all Carambus installations.

Tasks: 1. Scraping External Data - Loads data from ClubCloud (regional associations) - Loads data from billardarea.de (historical) - Imports tournament data, players, clubs, etc. - Updates rankings and results

  1. Central Data Storage
  2. Stores all data from all regions
  3. Complete database with all associations

  4. Master data source for synchronization

  5. Data Synchronization

  6. Distributes data to Local Servers
  7. Filters data by region (see below)
  8. Provides APIs for data queries

Hosting: - Typically on dedicated server (VPS/Cloud) - Runs 24/7 - High bandwidth for scraping - Large database (all regions)

Example: carambus.de (API Server for all of Germany)

docs.title: Local Server (Regional/Club Server)
docs.text:
A Local Server is a Carambus installation for a specific location (club, venue, region).

Tasks: 1. Local Game Management - Manages tournaments at location - Controls scoreboards in real-time - Local data capture

  1. Regional Data Storage
  2. Stores only data from own region (filtered!)
  3. Plus: Globally relevant data (DBU tournaments, etc.)

  4. Plus: Own Local Data (see below)

  5. Offline Capability

  6. Can work independently from API Server
  7. Important for match days (no internet dependency)
  8. Synchronizes later with API Server

Hosting: - Typically Raspberry Pi in club house - Or server at state association - Runs on local network - Smaller database (only own region)

Examples: - BC Hamburg Local Server (only Hamburg data + DBU tournaments) - BV Westfalen Local Server (only Westphalia data + DBU tournaments)

docs.title: 1. Scraping (API Server)
docs.text:
ClubCloud (NBV) → API Server → scraping → Database (all regions)
ClubCloud (BVW) → API Server → scraping → Database (all regions)
...

docs.title: 2. Synchronization (API → Local)
docs.text:
API Server (all data)
    ↓ Filter: region_id = Hamburg
Local Server Hamburg (only Hamburg data + Global)
    ↓ 
Scoreboards, Tournaments, etc.

docs.title: 3. Local Data Upload (Local → API)
docs.text:
Local Server Hamburg
    ↓ creates Tournament (Local Data, ID > 50,000,000)
    ↓ Upload to API Server
API Server (stores Local Data)
    ↓ Synchronization
Other Local Servers (receive Hamburg's Local Data)

docs.title: What is Local Data?
docs.text:
Local Data is data created on a Local Server that doesn't come from scraping by the API Server.

Simply put: Everything the club creates and manages itself, without being recorded in the supra-regional ClubCloud.

docs.title: ID Ranges
docs.text:
IDs:           1 - 49,999,999    → From API Server (scraped from ClubCloud)
IDs: 50,000,000 - 99,999,999    → Local Data (created locally at club)

Why important? - ✅ Local Server can work offline - ✅ No ID conflicts between servers - ✅ Clear distinction: External vs. Internal - ✅ Club has full control over own data

docs.title: 1. Club Tournament without ClubCloud Registration
docs.text:
Scenario: BC Hamburg organizes an internal year-end club tournament.

Problem:
- Tournament only takes place at club
- Should NOT be registered in ClubCloud (internal)
- Still needs Carambus for scoreboards and management

Solution with Local Data:
- Create tournament locally → ID: 50,012,345
- Full Carambus functionality
- Scoreboards, seeding, results
- Stays local, not in ClubCloud

docs.title: 2. Training Games for Performance Tracking
docs.text:
Scenario: Players want to track their development, but not enter every training session in ClubCloud.

Example BC Hamburg:
- Every Tuesday: Training with 8 players
- Capture via scoreboards
- Statistics: GD development, high series, etc.
- Local ranking for the club

Local Data:
- Training Games: IDs 50,100,001, 50,100,002, ...
- Only visible in club
- Long-term statistics possible
- Motivates players (measurable improvement!)

docs.title: 3. Guest Players at Club
docs.text:
Scenario: A player from another club visits regularly and participates in training.

Problem:
- Player is not a club member
- Not listed in ClubCloud as BC Hamburg player
- Should still be able to play on scoreboards

Solution:
- Create guest player locally → ID: 50,200,123
- Can participate in local games
- Does NOT appear in official lists
- Is NOT synchronized to API Server

docs.title: 4. Table Reservation with Heating Control
docs.text:
Scenario: The club has an automatic reservation system for billard tables with heating control.

Local Data Tables:
- Reservations: IDs 50,300,001, 50,300,002, ...
- Table occupancy
- Heating schedules
- Internal club management

Why local?
- Not relevant for other clubs
- No ClubCloud integration needed
- Faster access (local)
- Privacy (only club members)

docs.title: 5. Internal Club Championship over Multiple Weeks
docs.text:
Scenario: The club organizes a multi-week internal championship.

Setup:
- League (local): "BC Hamburg Winter Championship"
  ID: 50,400,001
- LeagueTeams (local): "The Veterans", "The Young Guns", etc.
  IDs: 50,400,010, 50,400,011, ...
- Parties (Match days): Every Friday
  IDs: 50,400,100, 50,400,101, ...
- PartyGames: Hundreds of games
  IDs: 50,400,500+

Advantage:
- Complete league management in club
- Not in official ClubCloud
- Own rules and modes possible
- Long-term statistics stored locally

docs.title: Created Locally (Local Server):
docs.text:
BC Hamburg Local Server
  ↓ Create tournament
  ID: 50,012,345 (Local Data)
  cc_id: NULL (not in ClubCloud)

docs.title: Optional: Upload to API Server
docs.text:
Local Server
  ↓ rake sync:to_api (if desired)
API Server
  ↓ stores with same ID
  ID: 50,012,345 is preserved!

docs.title: Synchronization to Other Local Servers
docs.text:
API Server (now has BC Hamburg Local Data)
  ↓ Synchronization (regionally filtered!)
Other NBV servers receive it
  ↓
BV Wedel Local Server sees:
  "BC Hamburg Club Tournament" (read-only)

Important: - Local Data remains protected (LocalProtector) - Only the creating server can edit - Other servers: read-only - No accidental overwrites

docs.title: 🎯 When to Use Local Data?
docs.text:
Use Local Data for: - ✅ Internal club tournaments - ✅ Training games and statistics - ✅ Guest players without club membership - ✅ Reservation systems - ✅ Internal leagues/championships - ✅ Everything that should NOT be in ClubCloud

Use scraped data for: - ✅ Official association tournaments - ✅ Bundesliga match days - ✅ Ranking tournaments - ✅ Regional championships - ✅ Everything that's in ClubCloud

docs.title: 📊 Comparison: API vs Local Server
docs.text: Aspect API Server Local Server Data Scope All regions Only own region + Global Database Size Large (~GB) Small (~MB) Scraping Yes, active No, only receives Internet Required Yes, always No, offline-capable Scoreboards Rare Yes, permanent Hosting VPS/Cloud Raspberry Pi, local Quantity 1 central Many (per club/region) Main Purpose Collect & distribute data Run match days

docs.title: Setup 1: Single Club
docs.text:
API Server (carambus.de)
    ↓ Synchronization (filtered)
Local Server (BC Hamburg, Raspberry Pi)
    ↓ LAN
Scoreboards (3 tables in club house)

docs.title: Setup 2: State Association
docs.text:
API Server (carambus.de)
    ↓ Synchronization (NBV data)
Local Server State Association (NBV server)
    ↓ Internet
    ├─ Local Server BC Hamburg (Raspberry Pi)
    ├─ Local Server BV Wedel (Raspberry Pi)
    └─ Local Server SC Pinneberg (Raspberry Pi)

docs.title: Setup 3: API Server Only (Development/Testing)
docs.text:
API Server (localhost:3000)
    ↓ direct
Browser (Developer, no scoreboards needed)

docs.title: Data Isolation
docs.text:
Advantage of regional filtering: - Hamburg sees no internal Westphalia data - Smaller database = faster queries - GDPR compliant (only relevant data)

Globally visible: - DBU tournaments (nationwide) - Rankings - Public events

Not global: - Internal club tournaments - Training parties - Local player details

docs.title: Problems without distributed system:
docs.text: ❌ Every club needs constant internet connection ❌ Central overload during many match days ❌ Single Point of Failure ❌ Slow scoreboards (remote access)

docs.title: Solution with API + Local Servers:
docs.text: ✅ Local Servers work offline ✅ Scoreboards are lightning-fast (LAN) ✅ Fail-safe (decentralized) ✅ Scalable (many Local Servers possible) ✅ Regional data stays regional

docs.title: Regional Filtering
docs.text:
# API Server has everything:
Tournament.count # => 5000 (all regions)

# Local Server Hamburg only has:
Tournament.where(region_id: nbv_id)
          .or(Tournament.where(global_context: true))
          .count # => 500 (only Hamburg + Global)

docs.title: Synchronization
docs.text:
From API → Local: 

# Rake task on Local Server:
rake sync:from_api[region_id]

# Loads:
# - All data with region_id = Hamburg
# - All data with global_context = true
# - All data with region_id = NULL

From Local → API: 

# Rake task on Local Server:
rake sync:to_api[local_data]

# Uploads:
# - All data with ID >= 50,000,000 (Local Data)
# - Newly created tournaments, players, etc.

docs.title: 📚 See Also
docs.text:
• Database Partitioning - Technical details
  • Scenario Management - Development with multiple scenarios
  • Installation - Setting up servers
  • Raspberry Pi Setup - Local Server on Raspberry Pi

docs.title: ❓ Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text:
Q: Can a club operate without an API Server? A: Yes! Local Server is fully functional without API Server. However, scraped data from ClubCloud will be missing.

Q: Does every club need a Local Server? A: No. Clubs can also use the API Server directly (via browser). Local Server is only needed for scoreboards and offline operation.

Q: What happens with ID conflicts? A: No conflicts possible: - API Server: IDs < 50,000,000 - Local Server: IDs >= 50,000,000 - Different ID ranges guarantee uniqueness

Q: Can a Local Server have data for multiple regions? A: Yes! A Local Server can filter multiple regions. Configurable via region_ids array.

Version: 1.0 Last Update: October 2024 Status: Production in Use

docs.title: Server Management Scripts
docs.text: This documentation describes all available scripts for managing Carambus servers (Development, Production, API).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Server Management Scripts are located in carambus_master/bin/ and cover the following areas: - Development Server: Start/manage local development environment - Production Server: Service management (Puma, Nginx) - Rails Console: Database access and debugging - Asset Management: Rebuild JavaScript/CSS - Utilities: Setup, restart, cleanup

docs.title: start-api-server.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Starts the API server (development mode)

Usage: 

cd carambus_master
./bin/start-api-server.sh

What it does: 1. ✅ Checks if port 3000 is free 2. ✅ Starts Puma server for carambus_api 3. ✅ Loads development configuration 4. ✅ Enables live reload

Prerequisites: - carambus_api_development database exists - Dependencies installed (bundle install) - Assets compiled

Access: 

http://localhost:3000

docs.title: start-local-server.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Starts a local scenario server (development mode)

Usage: 

./bin/start-local-server.sh <scenario_name>

What it does: 1. ✅ Changes to scenario directory 2. ✅ Reads port from config.yml 3. ✅ Starts Puma with scenario-specific config 4. ✅ Enables StimulusReflex/ActionCable

Examples: 

# Starts carambus_location_5101 on port 3003
./bin/start-local-server.sh carambus_location_5101

# Starts carambus_bcw on port 3007  
./bin/start-local-server.sh carambus_bcw

Prerequisites: - Scenario prepared with prepare_development - config.yml exists with webserver_port

docs.title: start-both-servers.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Starts API server and local server simultaneously

Usage: 

./bin/start-both-servers.sh <scenario_name>

What it does: - Starts API server (port 3000) in background - Starts scenario server (port from config.yml) - Both servers run in parallel

Use Cases: - Complete local testing - API sync testing - Development with multiple scenarios

Example: 

./bin/start-both-servers.sh carambus_location_5101
# API available: http://localhost:3000
# Location available: http://localhost:3003

Stopping: 

# Stop both servers
pkill -f puma

docs.title: manage-puma.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Manages Puma service on production server

Usage: 

# On the server:
./bin/manage-puma.sh [start|stop|restart|status]

# Remote via SSH:
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'cd /var/www/carambus_location_5101/current && ./bin/manage-puma.sh restart'

Actions: - start: Starts Puma service - stop: Stops Puma service cleanly - restart: Stops and starts (used by Capistrano) - status: Shows service status

What it does: 1. ✅ Checks systemd service status 2. ✅ Executes action 3. ✅ Waits for service start 4. ✅ Verifies socket/PID

Important: Called automatically by Capistrano, manual use rarely needed.

docs.title: manage-puma-api.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Manages Puma service for API server

Usage: 

./bin/manage-puma-api.sh [start|stop|restart|status]

Same functionality as manage-puma.sh, specifically for API server

docs.title: restart-carambus.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Quick restart for Carambus server

Usage: 

# Local
./bin/restart-carambus.sh

# Remote
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 '/var/www/carambus_location_5101/current/bin/restart-carambus.sh'

What it does: 1. ✅ Stops Puma service 2. ✅ Cleans PIDs/sockets 3. ✅ Restarts service 4. ✅ Waits for success

Use Cases: - Deploy code changes without Capistrano - After configuration changes - Quick restart on problems

docs.title: console-api.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Opens Rails console for API server

Usage: 

./bin/console-api.sh

What it does: - Changes to carambus_api - Starts Rails console (development) - Loads all models/helpers

Example Session: 

# Start script
./bin/console-api.sh

# In console:
> Player.count
=> 69082

> Version.last.id
=> 12227261

> Setting.find_by(key: 'last_version_id').value
=> "12227261"

docs.title: console-local.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Opens Rails console for local scenario

Usage: 

./bin/console-local.sh <scenario_name>

Examples: 

# Open scenario console
./bin/console-local.sh carambus_location_5101

# Check local data
> Game.where('id > 50000000').count
=> 28

> TableLocal.count
=> 10

docs.title: console-production.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Opens Rails console on production server

Usage: 

./bin/console-production.sh <scenario_name>

What it does: - SSH to production server - Changes to deployment directory - Starts Rails console (production)

⚠️ WARNING: Production console! Be careful with changes!

Example: 

# Open production console
./bin/console-production.sh carambus_location_5101

# Careful! Production data!
> Rails.env
=> "production"

> Game.count
=> 280163

Best Practice: - Never destructive operations without backup - Only read operations for debugging - For changes: Create migration

docs.title: rebuild_js.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Recompile JavaScript assets

Usage: 

cd carambus_location_5101
../carambus_master/bin/rebuild_js.sh

What it does: 1. ✅ yarn build (esbuild) 2. ✅ yarn build:css (TailwindCSS) 3. ✅ rails assets:precompile (Sprockets)

Use Cases: - After JavaScript changes - After CSS changes - Fix asset build errors

Example: 

# JavaScript changed, rebuild
cd carambus_location_5101
../carambus_master/bin/rebuild_js.sh

# Restart development server
./bin/start-local-server.sh carambus_location_5101

docs.title: cleanup_rails.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Clean Rails cache and temporary files

Usage: 

./bin/cleanup_rails.sh

What it does: - Deletes tmp/cache/ - Deletes .sprockets-cache/ - Deletes log/*.log (optional) - Cleans asset cache

Use Cases: - Fix asset problems - Free disk space - After large code changes

docs.title: cleanup_versions.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Cleans old version entries

Usage: 

./bin/cleanup_versions.sh [--dry-run]

What it does: - Deletes version entries older than X days - Keeps last N versions - Optional: Display only (--dry-run)

⚠️ WARNING: Only use on local servers! Not on API server!

docs.title: debug-production.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Collect debugging information from production

Usage: 

./bin/debug-production.sh <scenario_name>

What is collected: 1. ✅ Service status (Puma, Nginx) 2. ✅ Log files (last 100 lines) 3. ✅ Database status 4. ✅ Disk space 5. ✅ Memory usage 6. ✅ Process list

Output: 

./bin/debug-production.sh carambus_location_5101

=== Service Status ===
puma-carambus_location_5101.service: active (running)
nginx.service: active (running)

=== Logs (last 100 lines) ===
[...]

=== Database ===
Games: 280163
Players: 63972
Tournaments: 16748

=== Resources ===
Disk: 45% used
Memory: 678M / 8G

docs.title: carambus-install.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Complete Carambus installation on new server

Usage: 

./bin/carambus-install.sh

What is installed: 1. ✅ System dependencies (Ruby, Node, PostgreSQL) 2. ✅ Nginx + SSL 3. ✅ Redis (for ActionCable) 4. ✅ Git + SSH keys 5. ✅ Deployment user (www-data) 6. ✅ Directory structure

Prerequisites: - Fresh Ubuntu/Debian system - Root access - Internet connection

Duration: ~30 minutes

docs.title: setup-local-dev.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Set up local development environment

Usage: 

./bin/setup-local-dev.sh

What it does: 1. ✅ Checks system dependencies 2. ✅ Installs Ruby gems (bundle install) 3. ✅ Installs Node packages (yarn install) 4. ✅ Creates local database 5. ✅ Loads seed data 6. ✅ Compiles assets

docs.title: generate-ssl-cert.sh
docs.text:
Purpose: Generate SSL certificates for development/testing

Usage: 

./bin/generate-ssl-cert.sh [domain]

What it does: - Generates self-signed certificate - Creates private key - Saves in ssl/ directory

Use Cases: - Local HTTPS testing - Development with SSL features - Scoreboard testing with secure connection

Example: 

# Certificate for localhost
./bin/generate-ssl-cert.sh localhost

# Certificate for custom domain
./bin/generate-ssl-cert.sh carambus.local

docs.title: deploy.sh ⚠️
docs.text: Status: Obsolete (replaced by deploy-scenario.sh) Reason: Old deployment system without scenario support

docs.title: deploy-to-raspberry-pi.sh ⚠️
docs.text: Status: Obsolete (replaced by deploy-scenario.sh) Reason: Integrated into new scenario system

docs.title: puma-wrapper.sh ⚠️
docs.text: Status: Obsolete (replaced by manage-puma.sh) Reason: Outdated service management logic

docs.title: sync-carambus-folders.sh ⚠️
docs.text: Status: Obsolete Reason: Replaced by Git workflow

docs.title: Start Local Development Session
docs.text:
# 1. Prepare development environment
rake "scenario:prepare_development[carambus_location_5101,development]"

# 2. Build assets
cd carambus_location_5101
../carambus_master/bin/rebuild_js.sh

# 3. Start server
cd ../carambus_master
./bin/start-both-servers.sh carambus_location_5101

# API: http://localhost:3000
# Location: http://localhost:3003

docs.title: Deploy Code Change (Complete)
docs.text:
# 1. Commit code
git add .
git commit -m "Feature: XYZ"
git push carambus master

# 2. Update scenario
rake "scenario:update[carambus_location_5101]"

# 3. Rebuild assets
cd carambus_location_5101
../carambus_master/bin/rebuild_js.sh

# 4. Deploy
cd ../carambus_master
./bin/deploy-scenario.sh carambus_location_5101

# 5. Restart browser on RasPi
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 './bin/restart-scoreboard.sh'

docs.title: Quick Fix Without Complete Deployment
docs.text:
# 1. Commit small change
git commit -am "Fix: typo"
git push carambus master

# 2. Pull on production server
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107
cd /var/www/carambus_location_5101/current
git pull

# 3. Restart server
./bin/restart-carambus.sh
exit

# 4. Restart browser
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 './bin/restart-scoreboard.sh'

docs.title: Debug Production Problem
docs.text:
# 1. Collect debug info
./bin/debug-production.sh carambus_location_5101 > debug.log

# 2. Check logs
less debug.log

# 3. Open console (if needed)
./bin/console-production.sh carambus_location_5101

# 4. Apply quick fix
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo systemctl restart puma-carambus_location_5101'

docs.title: Puma Won't Start
docs.text:
# Problem: "Address already in use"
# Solution: Stop old processes
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107
pkill -9 puma
rm /var/www/carambus_location_5101/shared/pids/*.pid
rm /var/www/carambus_location_5101/shared/sockets/*.sock
./bin/manage-puma.sh start

docs.title: Assets Missing After Deployment
docs.text:
# Problem: "Asset not found"
# Solution: Recompile assets
cd carambus_location_5101
../carambus_master/bin/rebuild_js.sh

# On server:
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107
cd /var/www/carambus_location_5101/current
RAILS_ENV=production bundle exec rails assets:precompile
./bin/restart-carambus.sh

docs.title: Database Connection Fails
docs.text:
# Problem: "could not connect to server"
# Solution: Check PostgreSQL service
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107
sudo systemctl status postgresql
sudo systemctl start postgresql

# Check config
cat /var/www/carambus_location_5101/shared/config/database.yml

docs.title: Memory Problems
docs.text:
# Problem: "Cannot allocate memory"
# Solution: Memory analysis and cleanup
./bin/debug-production.sh carambus_location_5101 | grep Memory

# Clean cache
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107
cd /var/www/carambus_location_5101/current
./bin/cleanup_rails.sh

# Restart services
sudo systemctl restart puma-carambus_location_5101

docs.title: Development
docs.text:
1. ✅ Always start both servers for complete testing
  2. ✅ Run rebuild_js.sh after asset changes
  3. ✅ Use console for quick database checks
  4. ✅ Regularly run cleanup_rails.sh

docs.title: Production
docs.text:
1. ✅ Never manually start/stop Puma (use Capistrano)
  2. ✅ Console only for debugging, not for data changes
  3. ✅ On problems: First run debug-production.sh
  4. ✅ Check logs regularly

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
1. ✅ Complete deployment: deploy-scenario.sh
  2. ✅ Quick fixes: Only in emergencies
  3. ✅ After deployment: Restart browser on RasPi
  4. ✅ Before deployment: Local testing

docs.title: Daily Checks
docs.text:
# Service status
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'systemctl status puma-carambus_location_5101'

# Disk space
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'df -h'

# Logs (errors)
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'tail -100 /var/www/carambus_location_5101/current/log/production.log | grep ERROR'

docs.title: Weekly Maintenance
docs.text:
# 1. Clean Rails cache
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'cd /var/www/carambus_location_5101/current && ./bin/cleanup_rails.sh'

# 2. Rotate logs
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107 'sudo logrotate -f /etc/logrotate.d/carambus'

# 3. Check disk space
./bin/debug-production.sh carambus_location_5101 | grep "Disk:"

docs.title: Monthly Maintenance
docs.text:
# 1. System updates
ssh -p 8910 www-data@192.168.178.107
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

# 2. Update Ruby/Node/Gems
cd /var/www/carambus_location_5101/current
bundle update
yarn upgrade

# 3. Restart services
sudo systemctl restart puma-carambus_location_5101
sudo systemctl restart nginx

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Deployment Workflow - Complete deployment process
  • Scenario Management - Scenario system overview
  • Raspberry Pi Scripts - RasPi client management
  • Database Syncing - Database synchronization

docs.title: 1. Prepare Hardware (5 Min)
docs.text:
• Logitech C922 Webcam purchased and unpacked
  • Camera connected via USB to Scoreboard Raspi 4
  • Camera positioned above table (tripod/mount)
  • Raspi 4 running and reachable via SSH

Test: 

ping 192.168.1.100  # Replace with your Raspi IP

docs.title: 2. Prepare YouTube (10 Min)
docs.text:
• Open YouTube Studio: studio.youtube.com
  • Navigate: Settings → Stream
  • Create new stream key
  • Name: "Table 1 - My Location"
  • Copy key (e.g. xxxx-yyyy-zzzz-aaaa-bbbb)
  • ⚠️ Important: Wait 24h after first activation!

docs.title: 3. Setup Raspi (5 Min)
docs.text:
On the Location Server (Raspi 5):

cd /path/to/carambus_master

# Set SSH password
export RASPI_SSH_PASSWORD=raspberry  # Your actual password!

# Run setup
rake streaming:setup[192.168.1.100]  # Your Raspi IP

# Verify everything is OK
rake streaming:test[192.168.1.100]

Expectation: All tests ✅

docs.title: 4. Configure Stream (3 Min)
docs.text:
1. Open Carambus Admin Interface
  2. Navigate → YouTube Live Streaming
  3. New Stream Configuration

Minimal inputs: 

Table:              [Select your table]
YouTube Stream Key: [Paste from YouTube]
Raspi IP:           192.168.1.100

Rest: Default values OK for C922

  1. Click Save

docs.title: 5. Start Stream (1 Min)
docs.text:
1. In overview: Find Table 1
  2. Click ▶ Start
  3. Watch status: "Starting" → "Active"
  4. Open YouTube Studio → Stream should be live!

docs.title: Stream runs correctly when:
docs.text:
• Status in Admin Interface: 🟢 Active
  • Uptime counting up
  • YouTube Studio shows "Live"
  • Video shows billiard table
  • Scoreboard overlay visible (player names, score)
  • No error messages

docs.title: "Stream won't start"
docs.text:
# Check logs
ssh pi@192.168.1.100
sudo journalctl -u carambus-stream@1.service -f

docs.title: "Camera not found"
docs.text:
# Show camera devices
ssh pi@192.168.1.100
ls -l /dev/video*

If /dev/video1 instead of video0: → In Admin Interface configuration → Change camera device

docs.title: "YouTube shows nothing"
docs.text:
• Stream key copied correctly?
  • 24h waiting period after activation expired?
  • Firewall/Router blocking port 1935?

docs.title: 📖 More Information
docs.text:
Complete documentation: - docs/administrators/streaming-setup.en.md

Command reference: 

rake streaming:help

docs.title: 🎉 Done!
docs.text:
Your billiard table is now streaming live on YouTube!

Next steps: - Add more tables (repeat steps 4-5) - Optimize camera position - Adjust bitrate (if needed) - Enable automatic start

Good luck! 🎱📹

docs.title: 📋 Overview
docs.text: The Carambus system supports live streaming of billiard games directly to YouTube. It uses the existing Scoreboard Raspberry Pis to cost-effectively stream each table individually.

docs.title: Per Streamed Table
docs.text:
1. USB Webcam: Logitech C922 (~$80-90)
  2. 1280x720 @ 60fps (recommended for smooth movements)
  3. Alternative: Logitech C920 (~$60-70, 30fps)

  4. USB 2.0/3.0 connection

  5. Raspberry Pi 4 (already available as scoreboard)

  6. Minimum 2GB RAM (4GB recommended)

  7. OS: Raspberry Pi OS (Bullseye or newer)

  8. Camera Mount

  9. Tripod or wall mount
  10. USB extension cable (if needed)
  11. Position: Above the table, looking at playing surface

docs.title: Network Requirements
docs.text:
• Upload bandwidth: ~2-3 Mbit/s per stream at 720p60
  • Example: 4 parallel streams = ~10-12 Mbit/s upload needed
  • Stable LAN connection recommended (WiFi possible but not ideal)

docs.title: 1. Setup YouTube Channel
docs.text:
1. Sign in to YouTube
  2. Open YouTube Studio → studio.youtube.com
  3. Create channel (if not already existing)

docs.title: 2. Enable Live Streaming
docs.text:
1. YouTube Studio → Content → Live
  2. First activation: 24 hour waiting period
  3. After activation: Create stream keys

docs.title: 3. Generate Stream Key
docs.text:
1. YouTube Studio → Settings → Stream
  2. Create new stream key
  3. Name: e.g. "Table 1 - BC Hamburg"
  4. Copy stream key (format: xxxx-yyyy-zzzz-aaaa-bbbb)

Important: Create separate stream key per table!

docs.title: 4. Get Channel ID (optional)
docs.text:
1. YouTube Studio → Customization → Basic info
  2. Copy Channel ID (format: UCxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx)
  3. Needed for direct link to live stream

docs.title: 1. Prepare Raspberry Pi
docs.text:
On the Location Server (Raspi 5):

# Set SSH password as environment variable
export RASPI_SSH_USER=pi
export RASPI_SSH_PASSWORD=raspberry  # Replace with actual password!

# Run setup on Scoreboard Raspi
cd /path/to/carambus_master
rake streaming:setup[192.168.1.100]  # IP of Scoreboard Raspi

The setup script automatically installs: - FFmpeg (video encoding) - Xvfb (virtual framebuffer for overlay) - Chromium (overlay rendering) - v4l-utils (camera tools) - Systemd service files

docs.title: 2. Test Installation
docs.text:
rake streaming:test[192.168.1.100]

All tests should pass with ✅.

docs.title: 1. Create Stream Configuration
docs.text:
1. Open Carambus Admin Interface
  2. Navigate → YouTube Live Streaming (or /admin/stream_configurations)
  3. Click New Stream Configuration

docs.title: 2. Basic Settings
docs.text:
Table: - Select location and table

YouTube Configuration: - Stream Key: Copy from YouTube - Channel ID: (optional) For direct link

docs.title: 3. Camera Settings
docs.text:
Recommended values for Logitech C922: 

Device:      /dev/video0
Width:       1280
Height:      720
Framerate:   60 fps

For Logitech C920: 

Framerate:   30 fps  (rest same)

docs.title: 4. Overlay Settings
docs.text:
Overlay enabled:  ✓
Position:         Bottom
Height:           200 px

The overlay shows: - Player names - Current score - Tournament info (if available) - Live indicator

docs.title: 5. Stream Quality
docs.text:
Recommended values: 

Video bitrate:  2000 kbit/s  (720p60)
Audio bitrate:  128 kbit/s

Adjustments based on upload: - More bandwidth: 2500 kbit/s - Less bandwidth: 1500 kbit/s

docs.title: 6. Network
docs.text:
Raspi IP:       192.168.1.100  (automatically taken from table)
SSH port:       22

docs.title: 7. Save & Deploy
docs.text:
1. Click Save
  2. Configuration is deployed to Scoreboard Raspi
  3. Check status: Should be "Inactive"

docs.title: Via Admin Interface (recommended)
docs.text:
1. Open /admin/stream_configurations
  2. Find desired stream
  3. Click Start
  4. Status changes to "Starting" → "Active"
  5. On error: Error message is displayed

docs.title: Via SSH (manual)
docs.text:
ssh pi@192.168.1.100
sudo systemctl start carambus-stream@1.service

# Check status
sudo systemctl status carambus-stream@1.service

# View logs
sudo journalctl -u carambus-stream@1.service -f

docs.title: Via Rake Task
docs.text:
cd /path/to/carambus_master
rake streaming:status  # Show all streams

docs.title: Check Stream Status
docs.text:
In Admin Interface: - Live status display - Uptime counter - Error messages - Click Health Check for current diagnosis

Via Rake Task: 

rake streaming:status

Via SSH: 

ssh pi@192.168.1.100

# Service status
sudo systemctl status carambus-stream@1.service

# Live logs
sudo journalctl -u carambus-stream@1.service -f

# Check FFmpeg process
ps aux | grep ffmpeg
